skill.name: flow-tdd
skill.description: Guides an AI coding assistant through strict test-driven development with failing tests before implementation.
skill.summary: Enforces a red-green-refactor workflow for AI-assisted development.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged backticks, relative paths, weak-crypto labels, and reconnaissance labels in SKILL.md. Review found these are false positives from Markdown examples, documentation links, and testing language; no executable script, network call, credential access, or prompt injection was found. The main residual risk is destructive workflow guidance that tells an AI agent to delete or comment out existing implementation code during TDD recovery.

medium_findings.title: Destructive TDD Recovery Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends deleting implementation code or commenting it out when code was written before tests. This is aligned with strict TDD practice, but an AI agent could remove useful work without explicit user approval or version control safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive instructions are explicit and repeated. The context is legitimate TDD recovery, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick detections are Markdown code fences and examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The skill does instruct agents to run project tests, which is expected for a TDD workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows fenced documentation blocks, not code that the skill can execute. The only command-like text is test-running guidance.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Are Not Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections are Markdown cross-reference links to related skill and rule documents. No file-read operation, dynamic path construction, or traversal exploit was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious ../ sequences appear only inside Markdown links. There is no executable code path using these strings.

low_findings.title: Crypto and Reconnaissance Labels Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and reconnaissance detections map to ordinary TDD prose, test examples, and headings. No hashing, encryption, host probing, port scanning, or network discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language testing guidance and sample test output. They do not contain crypto APIs or reconnaissance commands.

low_findings.title: Error Log File Creation Requires Workspace Scope
low_findings.description: The skill tells agents to create or append ERROR_LOG.md under a requirement directory. This is useful process documentation, but marketplace users should expect workspace file writes when applying the workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write instruction is clear, but it targets a project-local error log. The risk depends on whether the agent asks before creating or appending files.

content.value_statement: AI coding sessions often drift into implementation before tests exist. This skill keeps the assistant on a strict TDD loop with failing tests, minimal implementation, and error logging.
actual_capabilities: Requires a failing test before production code is written.
Guides the red, green, and refactor stages of TDD.
Defines checkpoints for verifying new tests fail before implementation.
Provides rules for handling code that was written before tests.
Adds an error recording workflow for failed tests and build errors.
Links the TDD process to related project constitution and recovery documents.
limitations: It is an instruction-only skill and does not include runnable test tooling.
It assumes the project already has a test command and test framework.
Its strict delete-and-restart advice may be too aggressive for some teams.
Some references point to neighboring project files that may not exist in other repositories.

use_cases.title: Start a New Feature With Tests
use_cases.description: Use the skill to force acceptance, integration, or unit tests before implementation begins.

use_cases.title: Recover From Test-After Coding
use_cases.description: Use the recovery rules when implementation already exists and the team wants to return to test-first work.

use_cases.title: Improve AI Coding Discipline
use_cases.description: Use the checkpoints to keep Claude, Codex, or Claude Code from adding untested production behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Begin a TDD Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a new change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use flow-tdd for this feature. First identify the behavior, then write a failing test before any production code.

prompt_templates.title: Verify the Red Phase
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply flow-tdd and show me the new test result before implementation. If the test passes immediately, stop and explain why it is invalid.

prompt_templates.title: Implement One Passing Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for the green phase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Continue with flow-tdd. Implement only the minimum code needed to make the current failing test pass, then run the relevant tests.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Existing Change for TDD Compliance
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for reviewing work already done.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this branch with flow-tdd. Identify any production code that lacks a prior failing test, propose recovery steps, and avoid deleting code without approval.

output_examples.input: Add email validation to the signup flow using TDD.
output_examples.output: The assistant defines invalid email behavior, writes a failing validation test, confirms the red result, then adds minimal validation logic.

output_examples.input: I already wrote the implementation. Bring it into TDD compliance.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the test-after risk, asks before removing or disabling implementation, then creates tests that fail before restoring behavior.

output_examples.input: A new test failed during implementation.
output_examples.output: The assistant records the failure context, analyzes the root cause, fixes the behavior, and updates the error log after the tests pass.

best_practices: Use version control before applying recovery steps that remove or comment out code.
Run the smallest relevant test set during red and green phases, then run broader tests before finishing.
Ask for explicit approval before deleting existing implementation code.
anti_patterns: Do not write production code before a failing test exists.
Do not accept tests that pass immediately for newly requested behavior.
Do not use broad mocks or placeholder assertions that fail to check real behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests by itself?
faq.answer: No. It instructs the assistant to run the project test command, but it does not ship executable tooling.

faq.question: Is this compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it be used without an existing test framework?
faq.answer: It can guide the workflow, but the project still needs a real test framework and command.

faq.question: Why does it require a failing test first?
faq.answer: A failing test proves the requested behavior is not already satisfied and gives implementation a clear target.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The skill contains strict recovery guidance that may delete or comment out existing code if followed without approval.

faq.question: Should this skill be used on uncommitted work?
faq.answer: Use caution. Commit, stash, or otherwise protect work before applying recovery steps that alter existing files.
